questions.question: Which of the following acts of the East India Company mandated that selection for civil employees in British Indian territory would be based on the competitive examinations?
questions.solution:
Competitive Examinations

The Charter Act of 1853 was a landmark for the Indian Civil Service (ICS). It introduced an open competitive system for recruitment, which effectively stripped the East India Company's Court of Directors of their patronage powers. Following this, the Macaulay Committee was appointed in 1854 to implement the reforms, eventually allowing Indians to compete for high-level administrative posts.

questions.question: What is Lord Thomas Babington Macaulay best known for in the context of British India?
questions.option_3: Framing the Government of India Act, 1858
questions.option_4: Drafting the Indian Penal Code and promoting English education
questions.solution:
Lord Macaulay's Legacy

Lord Thomas Babington Macaulay is primarily remembered for two major contributions: the Indian Penal Code (1835), which sought to codify Indian law, and his Minute on Education. He famously advocated for an education system that would create a class of persons "Indian in blood and colour, but English in taste, in opinions, in morals, and in intellect," leading to the promotion of English-medium education.

questions.question: British land revenue policies were deeply exploitative. Under which system were landlords required to pay a fixed revenue regardless of crop yield?
questions.solution:
Zamindari System

The Zamindari System, also known as the Permanent Settlement, was introduced by Lord Cornwallis in 1793 in Bengal and Bihar. Under this system, the land revenue was fixed permanently. Even if crops were destroyed by drought or floods, the Zamindars were legally bound to pay the fixed amount to the British, which led to immense pressure on the peasantry and the frequent sale of lands for non-payment.

questions.question: Commercial crops were prioritised over food crops during colonial rule. Which of the following is an example of a commercial crop promoted by the British?
questions.solution:
Commercial Crops

The British transformed Indian agriculture from subsistence-based to market-oriented. They incentivized or forced farmers to grow Indigo (highly valued as a blue dye for the British textile industry), jute, cotton, and opium. This commercialization often occurred at the expense of food security, contributing to the severity of local famines.

questions.question: During the colonial period, India's industrial development was the result of British policies. Which of the following options best describes the nature of industrial development in India at the eve of independence?
questions.option_1: Limited industrial activity, mainly concentrated in a few cities like Calcutta and Bombay
questions.option_2: Widespread industrial growth across rural and urban areas
questions.option_3: Balanced regional distribution of heavy industries
questions.option_4: Strong domestic capital goods industry in all provinces
questions.solution:
Industrial Development at Independence

On the eve of independence, India's industrial profile was starkly limited. Development was restricted to light consumer industries like cotton and jute textiles, with most factories clustered in Bombay and Calcutta. There was no meaningful development of capital goods industries (machinery), leaving the new nation heavily dependent on imports for industrial growth.

questions.question: The British used education as a tool to ______.
questions.solution:
Education as a Colonial Tool

Western education was not introduced primarily for social reform, but to strengthen colonial rule. By training a limited number of Indians in English and Western administrative methods, the British created a loyal bureaucratic class capable of handling the day-to-day administrative tasks of the Raj at a much lower cost than British officials.

questions.question: The Indian textile industry was subjected to discriminatory trade practices. Which of the following policies harmed the Indian textile industry under British rule?
questions.option_1: Equal tariffs on imports and exports
questions.option_2: Imposition of heavy duties on Indian exports to Britain
questions.solution:
Trade Practices and Textiles

The British policy toward the Indian textile industry is a classic example of de-industrialization. They practiced one-way free trade: British machine-made cloth entered India with almost no duties, while heavy duties were imposed on Indian exports to Britain. This rendered Indian hand-crafted textiles uncompetitive, leading to the collapse of once-thriving weaving centers like Dacca and Murshidabad.

questions.question: Which colonial power held Yanaon (Yanam) and Chandernagore as part of its possessions in India?
questions.solution:
French Possessions

While the British held the majority of the subcontinent, the French maintained several coastal enclaves until the mid-20th century. These included Pondicherry (the headquarters), Chandernagore in Bengal, Yanaon in Andhra Pradesh, Karikal in Tamil Nadu, and Mahe in Kerala.

questions.question: Who is most commonly associated with the 'safety valve' theory regarding the formation of the Indian National Congress?
questions.solution:
The Safety Valve Theory

The 'Safety Valve' theory posits that A.O. Hume, a retired British civil servant, acted as a catalyst for forming the INC under the secret guidance of Viceroy Lord Dufferin. The idea was that the Congress would serve as a platform to vent Indian political frustrations peacefully, thereby preventing another mass explosion of anger like the Revolt of 1857.

questions.question: Which of the following statements is/are correct regarding the Dandi March of 1930? (a) The march started from Sabarmati Ashram and ended at Dandi, covering a distance of 240 miles. (b) The Dandi March led to the signing of the Poona Pact.
questions.solution:
Dandi March (1930)

The Dandi March was the inaugural event of the Civil Disobedience Movement. Mahatma Gandhi and 78 followers walked 240 miles from Sabarmati Ashram to the coastal village of Dandi to manufacture salt in defiance of the British monopoly. The Poona Pact (1932), conversely, was an agreement between Gandhi and Dr. Ambedkar regarding communal representation and had no direct link to the march itself.

questions.question: Nagara Uprising, a significant peasant rebellion that occurred in the Nagar province (present-day Shivamogga district) of the Mysore Kingdom, took place in:
questions.solution:
Nagara Uprising

The Nagara Uprising (1830-31) was a major peasant revolt in the Mysore Kingdom. It was triggered by oppressive land revenue systems and the corruption of local 'Amildars.' The rebellion grew so large that the British had to intervene directly, which eventually led to the British taking over the administration of Mysore from the Maharaja for several decades.

questions.question: In which year did the Dutch East India Company establish its factory at Petapuli on the North Coromandel Coast?
questions.solution:
Dutch Factory at Petapuli

In 1606, the Dutch East India Company established its first trading posts on the Coromandel Coast at Petapuli (modern Nizampatnam) and Masulipatnam. The Dutch were particularly interested in the region's textiles (chintz and calico), which they traded in the Spice Islands for cloves and nutmeg.

questions.question: Which of the following companies assisted the Raja of Cochin in expelling the Portuguese from his kingdom in 1663?
questions.solution:
Expelling the Portuguese

By the mid-17th century, the Dutch aggressively challenged Portuguese maritime supremacy. In 1663, they allied with the Raja of Cochin to capture the Portuguese forts in the region. This victory effectively ended Portuguese influence on the Malabar Coast and established the Dutch as the dominant European power in the Indian pepper trade for a time.

questions.question: Which leader collaborated with Mahatma Gandhi during the Kheda Satyagraha in 1918?
questions.solution:
Kheda Satyagraha

The Kheda Satyagraha of 1918 was Gandhi's second major movement in India. He was joined by Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel, who played a crucial role in organizing the farmers of the Kheda district to refuse revenue payments following a total crop failure, defying the British government's rigid tax laws.

questions.question: Which of the following can be considered as an immediate cause of the Sanyasi Rebellion?
questions.option_1: Development of a broad sense of view of Indian Nationalism
questions.option_2: Restrictions imposed by the British upon pilgrims visiting holy places among both Hindus and Muslims
questions.option_3: British occupation of Madras region and their new land laws along with the atrocities of the landlords
questions.option_4: Implementation of the Chota Nagpur Tenancy Act of 1908
questions.solution:
Sanyasi Rebellion

The Sanyasi Rebellion (1763–1800) in Bengal was driven by multiple factors, including the Famine of 1770. However, a major immediate cause was the restrictions imposed by the British on pilgrims (both Hindu Sanyasis and Muslim Madari Faqirs) visiting holy sites. Bankim Chandra Chatterjee’s famous novel Anandamath is set against the backdrop of this revolt.

questions.question: In which early INC session was the demand first raised for the Indianisation of the higher administrative services, including the civil services?
questions.solution:
Bombay Session, 1885

The first session of the Indian National Congress was held at Gokuldas Tejpal Sanskrit College in Bombay (1885). One of the earliest resolutions passed was the demand for the Indianisation of the Civil Services, emphasizing that administrative examinations should be held simultaneously in India and England to give Indians a fair chance at governance.

questions.question: According to the Regulating Act of 1773 of the East India Company, to whom did the governors of Madras and Bombay report regularly?
questions.solution:
Regulating Act of 1773

The Regulating Act of 1773 introduced the first major change in the Company's structure. It created the office of the Governor-General of Bengal and made the Governors of Bombay and Madras subordinate to him. They were required to report to and take orders from the Governor-General, particularly concerning foreign policy and war.

questions.question: Which of the following was formed in 1600 through a charter that granted it the exclusive privilege of trading with the area east of the Cape of Good Hope for 15 years?
questions.solution:
English East India Company

The English East India Company received its Royal Charter from Queen Elizabeth I on December 31, 1600. This charter granted the company a 15-year monopoly on trade with all countries east of the Cape of Good Hope, protecting them from competition by other English merchants.

questions.question: The 'Drain of Wealth' theory, which highlighted the economic exploitation of India under British rule, was prominently advocated by whom?
questions.solution:
Drain of Wealth Theory

Dadabhai Naoroji pioneered the 'Drain of Wealth' theory. He argued that Britain was extracting India's wealth through various means—such as the 'Home Charges,' salaries of British officials, and trade profits—without providing any equivalent economic return. He quantified this as the primary cause of India's recurring famines and persistent poverty.

questions.question: The enactment of the first Cooperative Credit Societies Act in 1904 was influenced by which of the following?
questions.solution:
Cooperative Credit Societies Act

The Cooperative Credit Societies Act of 1904 was passed following the recommendations of the Famine Commission of 1901. The Commission realized that agrarian distress was exacerbated by high-interest debt. The Act aimed to encourage the formation of small, local cooperative societies that could provide farmers with cheap credit and promote thrift.

questions.question: Which movement or idea is associated with the Wardha Scheme of Basic Education proposed in 1937?
questions.solution:
Nai Talim (Basic Education)

The Wardha Scheme, also known as Nai Talim, was Mahatma Gandhi’s vision for a self-sufficient education system. It proposed that education should be centered around a craft (like spinning or weaving) and taught in the mother tongue, making it accessible and practical for the millions of people living in India's villages.

questions.question: Which of the following Acts allowed the Crown to take over governance from the East India Company?
questions.solution:
Government of India Act 1858

The 1858 Act, often called the 'Act for the Better Government of India,' was a direct result of the 1857 Revolt. It abolished the rule of the East India Company and transferred all administrative and military powers to the British Crown. The Governor-General was given the title of Viceroy, representing the Crown in India.

questions.question: Which of the following companies founded in 1602 is also known as the 'Verenigde Oostindische Compagnie (VOC)'?
questions.solution:
The VOC

The Dutch East India Company was formed in 1602 through the merger of several rival Dutch trading companies. It was known in Dutch as the Verenigde Oostindische Compagnie (VOC). It became the world's first publicly listed company and was a dominant force in the spice trade for nearly two centuries.

questions.question: Which power suffered a decisive defeat in the Malabar region during the Battle of Colachel in 1741?
questions.solution:
Battle of Colachel

The Battle of Colachel (1741) was a historic victory for the Indian state of Travancore, led by Maharaja Marthanda Varma, against the Dutch East India Company. This defeat broke the Dutch monopoly over the black pepper trade and effectively ended Dutch territorial ambitions in South India.

questions.question: Who among the following established its factory in Petapuli on the North Coromandel Coast in 1606?
questions.solution:
Dutch in Petapuli

Establishing a factory at Petapuli in 1606 gave the Dutch a strategic foothold on the Coromandel Coast. This region was the center of the calico (cotton cloth) industry, which the Dutch exported to Europe and the East Indies, often outperforming the English in the early 17th-century trade race.
